coredata.dc:title: pH-dependent fluorescent quenching of graphene oxide quantum dots: Towards hydroxyl
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Huanhuan
Xu, Ang
Feng, Zhijun
Long, Deng
Chen, Xinyi
Lu, Miao
coredata.dc:description: The photoluminescence of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) is known to be quenched by metal ions. In this paper, this quenching effect has been found to be strengthened with the increase of environmental pH, which would introduce hydroxyl group for GOQDs because other kinds of functional groups have the trend to evolve towards hydroxyl with increasing pH. Thus, a pH-dependent fluorescent quenching of GOQDs can be obtained, which might further guide the metal ion detection of GOQDs in a practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Functional groups

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of the GOQDs. (Ref. JCPDS: 41-1487).
(a) TEM image and (b) size distribution of the GOQDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of GOQDs; XPS of (b) GOQDs and the fine structure at (c) C1s; (d) O1s.
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) PL of GOQDs; Fe involved evolution of GOQDs PL (excitated at 450 nm) with (c) stabilizing time and (d) Fe concentration.

(a) Fluorescent quenching of GOQDs with varying environmental pH (F0 and F are the PL intensities of GOQDs without and with Fe, respectively); (b) FTIR spectra and (c) fluorescent lifetime of GOQDs; (d) PL and FTIR spectra (the inset) of annealed GOQDs.

head.ce:title: pH-dependent fluorescent quenching of graphene oxide quantum dots: Towards hydroxyl

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pen-Tung Sah Institute of Micro-Nano Science & Technology, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pen-Tung Sah Institute of Micro-Nano Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pen-Tung Sah Institute of Micro-Nano Science & Technology, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photoluminescence of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) is known to be quenched by metal ions. In this paper, this quenching effect has been found to be strengthened with the increase of environmental pH, which would introduce hydroxyl group for GOQDs because other kinds of functional groups have the trend to evolve towards hydroxyl with increasing pH. Thus, a pH-dependent fluorescent quenching of GOQDs can be obtained, which might further guide the metal ion detection of GOQDs in a practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and their derivatives such as graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) have exhibited great potential in the fields of energy, bio-imaging and environment due to the spectroscopic engineering, size confinement, edge effect and chemical functionalization. The photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs, comprehensively featured by size, shape, edge, dopants and functional groups, is one of the most attractive properties that has been widely studied for the detection of metal ions in the field of environment [1–5]. A typical mechanism of PL based detection mainly experiences the following processes: i) photoinduced electron transition and the corresponding fluorescent transition (unexposed to quencher); ii) the formation of a stable complex between fluorophores and the quencher; iii) quenching of the corresponding emissions. For GQDs, there are two kinds of fluorophores contributing to the PL i.e. the size related intrinsic state and defect states, while the defect states i.e. oxygenated functional groups dominate the PL of GOQDs [6,7].

In a real detection, the varieties of ions, organic components, as well as the acidity would be much more complicated than those in a single system, affecting the formation between the targets and fluorophores in GQDs or GOQDs and thus weakening the accuracy, selectivity and sensitivity. The native GQDs and GOQDs show good selectivity i.e. highly sensitive to Fe attributed to the binding between Fe and hydroxyl [8]. However, the pH of target solutions also influences the dispersity and functional groups of GQDs and GOQDs and further the interaction between Fe and graphene, leading to the variety of Fe induced fluorescent quenching. Also, a practical field test usually employs a monochromatic characterization, saving the continuous light source and spectrometer for portability and low energy consumption. With that in mind, the influence of environmental pH on Fe induced fluorescent quenching of GOQDs at a fixed emission wavelength has been detailly studied. It has been found that the Fe induced fluorescent quenching at 537 nm was strengthened with increasing pH from 2 to 12, indicating that the content of hydroxyl increased with the pH, while other functional groups inert to Fe, existing more in acidic conditions and contributing more to the 537 nm emission, had the trend to be replaced by hydroxyl with the increase of environmental pH.

The GOQDs were extracted from a modified Hummers method derived GO [9,10], using expanded graphite (0.5 g), NaNO3 (0.5 g) and KMnO4 (3 g) in 60 mL concentrated H2SO4 (98%), stirred for 40 h and dissolved in 200 mL deionized water, followed by dropwise adding H2O2. Then the solution was sonicated for 2 h to break big GO sheets and centrifugated repeatedly to get rid of the impurities. Then another series of centrifugation from 6000 rpm to 10000 rpm was gradually taken to extract the supernate i.e. GOQDs. The as-prepared GOQDs solution has a concentration of about 0.3 mg/mL and the pH of about 2.1.

The morphology, structure and optical properties of GOQDs was characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100), X-ray diffractometer (XRD, XRD-7000), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantum-2000), UV–Vis absorption (UV/V-16/18), PL spectroscopy (F-7000) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet is 10). To investigate the optical properties of GOQDs as well as their fluorescent quenching in different conditions with varying pH, a buffer solution was prepared by mixing 0.01 mol/L Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 with different volume ratios, while a pH meter was applied to monitor the pH. Then 100 μL as-prepared GOQDs solution was added into 1 mL buffer solution with a certain pH before optical characterization.

X-Ray diffraction pattern of the GOQDs as shown in Fig. 1 exhibited a peak at 2θ = 9.7° corresponding to the interlayer spacing (~9.709 Å) of GO stacking along [002] direction, which could be attributed to oxygen related functional groups at the edges, the basal plane of GO, or water molecules trapped between the sheets [9,11]. In addition, there was a tiny and broad reflection close to 20°, which was also attributed to the presence of GO [10].

The TEM image shown in Fig. 2 (a) the morphology of the as-prepared GOQDs, while the extracted size distribution shown in Fig. 2(b) was ranging from 1.1 nm to 3.9 nm with an average diameter of ~2.32 nm, indicating that the gradual centrifugation guaranteed the size purification of GOQDs. The FTIR of GOQDs shown in Fig. 3 (a) exhibited a strong peak at 3426 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl [12], while peaks between 1000 cm−1 and 2000 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of CC of aromatic ring, COH/COO of carboxylic groups, OH of phenolic, CO and CO of carboxylic groups, etc.[12–14] It should be noticed that there was a weak peak at 1275 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of epoxy [12,14]. Correspondingly, C 1s peak of GOQDs in XPS (Fig. 3(b, c)) could be identified to CC (284.69 eV), CC (285.4 eV), CO (286.9 eV), CO (288.2 eV), and OCO (289 eV) [15,16], with the percentages of 39.426%, 11.725%, 36.111%, 5.205% and 7.533%, respectively. Meanwhile, the O 1s peak (Fig. 2(b, d)) could be fitted to two peaks at 531.8 eV (CO) and 532.7 eV (COH/COC) [11], respectively.

The UV–vis absorption of the GOQDs shown in Fig. 4 (a) exhibited a strong absorption peak at 230 nm related to π-π* electronic transition of aromatic C-sp2, and a shoulder at 300 nm due to n-π* transition of CO [17,18]. PL spectra shown in Fig. 4(b) exhibited the typical excitation-wavelength-dependent emission properties of GOQDs with the excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 500 nm [19,20], which might indicate the presence of various oxygenated functional groups [21]. Since the maximum PL intensity was observed under the excitation at 450 nm, this wavelength was further employed in the analysis on fluorescent quenching of GOQDs. Meanwhile, the PL intensity of GOQDs exhibited a 31 percent decade in 92 days (Fig. S1). For GOQDs, defect states dominated the PL [6], indicating a proper stability of the functional groups in as-prepared GOQDs.

While added Fe (20 μM), the PL intensity sharply declined about 62 percent in the first 30 s and stabilized in about 2.5 min as shown in Fig. 4(c). Generally, GOQDs exhibited rapid response, high stability and good selectivity (Figs. S1 and S2). The PL intensity was quenched gradually with the increasing Fe concentrations (the ratio of Fe3+and Fe2+ was 1:1, Fig. S3), as shown in Fig. 4(d). Notably, the Fe concentration induced PL quenching showed a good linear relationship ranging from 9.9 nM to 81.47 μΜ that could be fitted into Ln(F0/F) = 0.0115*[Fe] + 0.05292, where F0 and F were the PL intensity in the absence and presence of Fe, respectively. As a result, a relatively high sensitivity compared to other fluorescent carbon dots based Fe detection, i.e. 4.97 nM could be extracted (Fig. S4). Practically, the Fe concentration in the domestic tap water was determined to be 4.77 μΜ via fluorescent quenching effect, similar to that of 4.66 μΜ via the commonly used o-diazophene spectrophotometry [22] (Fig. S5). The slow PL aging, high sensitivity and accuracy in Fe detection, showed the stability of functional groups in GOQDs and the corresponding interaction with Fe at moderate concentrations.

The Fe (20 μM) induced fluorescent quenching at 2.5 min of GOQDs was found to be strengthened with the increase of pH as Fig. 5 (a) suggested, indicating an improved interaction between –OH of GOQDs and Fe [8]. The FTIR spectra of pH-varied GOQDs shown in Fig. 5(b) and Fig. S6(a) established the presence of epoxy and hydroxyl of GOQDs in acidic environment, while the content of hydroxyl increased with the increase of environmental pH and the presence of epoxy could be hardly identified in alkaline conditions. On the other hand, for the Raman spectra of pH-varied GOQDs as shown in Fig. S6, the ratio of D peak (representing the disordered graphitic structure) to G peak (representing ideal graphitic structure) did not change obviously with the increase of environmental pH, which might suggest that the oxygenated functional groups of GOQDs were not reduced. Those oxygenated functional groups evolved with the change of environmental pH and the dominative functional group would be different in different pH condition. The introduction of Fe made the hydroxyl of GOQDs interact with Fe and quenched the hydroxyl featured fluorescence, which also weakened the original hydroxyl vibration mode under FTIR as Fig. 5(b) indicated. It should be noticed that, for the emission at about 537 nm excitated by 450 nm, the strengthened Fe induced fluorescent quenching with the increase of environmental pH was also along with the decrease of PL intensity of pure GOQDs as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(a), implicating that the increased quantity of hydroxyl did not contribute to the emission at 537 nm i.e. this emission would be dominated by other oxygenated functional groups. The previous study had found that hydroxyl could act as an electron donator to improve the quantum yield and thus the intensity of blue emission of GQDs [23]. On the contrary, the green emission of GOQDs would be eliminated during reduction, which was attributed to the elimination of epoxy and generation of hydroxyl [24]. Correspondingly, the fluorescent lifetime of Fe incorporated GOQDs slightly differed from pure GOQDs as shown in Fig. 5(c), also indicating an Fe induced change of fluorophores in GOQDs. Since the investigation of Fe induced fluorescent quenching in this work was taken at the emission of 537 nm, the observed decrease of PL intensity at 537 nm with the increase of pH was consistent with the previous study. Furthermore, our previous study found the evolution of functional groups towards hydroxyl during annealing for graphene oxide [10]. For GOQDs, the PL intensity at 537 nm was found to decrease as the annealing temperature increased as shown in Fig. 5d, similar to that with the increase of environmental pH. So it can be deduced that the hydroxyl featured fluorescence, which could be quenched by Fe, was not the dominative fluorophore for the emission at 537 nm, while other oxygenated functional groups were gradually replaced by hydroxyl with the increase of environmental pH. Eventually, the evolution of functional groups resulted in such a pH-dependent fluorescent quenching of GOQDs at 537 nm: with the increase of environmental pH, the PL intensity decreased but the fluorescent quenching effect of Fe became more obvious.

The metal ion induced fluorescent quenching of GOQDs is found to be strengthened with the increase of environmental pH. With the increase of pH, the functional groups of GOQDs have the trend to evolve towards hydroxyl, which can contribute to the quenching by reacting with the quencher such as Fe. As a result, the fluorescent quenching can be strengthened with the increasing hydroxyl i.e. the environmental pH.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (61071010), and the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (No. 2015J05130). We thank Ms. Lingling Zheng and Mr. Jiaqin Wang for the technical support.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings are available on request.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2020.114627.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
